task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
The blinking light was a reflector on the boot of the poacher, McLeach, who stepped out from behind a tree with a cruel grin.

“Well, well. The little brat from the posters. Knew you’d come sniffin’ around a trapped animal,” McLeach growled, his voice like grinding stones. He loomed over the pit, his shadow swallowing Cody.

“Let him go, McLeach!” Faloo screeched, swooping down from a branch. The kookaburra’s call was echoed by a chorus of chirps and squeaks from the surrounding bushes, where the other animals had gathered, hidden but defiant.

McLeach just laughed, a harsh, barking sound. “Or what? You’ll laugh me to death?” He reached down, his large hand closing around Cody’s arm, and hauled him unceremoniously out of the trap. Cody kicked and struggled, but the poacher’s grip was like iron.

“You’re a persistent little pest, ain’t ya?” McLeach said, holding Cody up. “But you just did me a favor. See, I’ve been tryin’ to bag that golden eagle for years. She’s too smart for my traps. But she’s not too smart for sentiment.”

He shook Cody slightly. “She likes you. I saw the whole little reunion. So now, you’re gonna be my new bait.” He dumped Cody into a dirty canvas sack and slung it over his shoulder. “A squeakin’, wrigglin’ dinner bell.”

From inside the sack, Cody heard Faloo’s desperate cry and the frantic chattering of the other animals. The world jolted as McLeach began to walk. “Don’t you worry about your furry friends,” McLeach muttered. “They follow, they get a face full of buckshot.”

The journey was rough. Cody could feel every step, hear the crunch of dry leaves and the distant, fading calls of his friends. He fought down a wave of panic, thinking of Marahute’s eggs, of his mom expecting him for supper. He had to get loose.

After what felt like hours, the jolting stopped. Cody was dumped onto a hard, metal surface. The sack opened, and the harsh light of a kerosene lamp made him blink. He was in the back of McLeach’s battered truck, parked in a dusty canyon clearing. In the distance, the silhouette of Marahute’s cliff was visible against the twilight sky.

McLeach tied Cody’s hands and ankles with rough rope, then secured the rope to a heavy metal ring bolted to the truck bed. “You sit tight, junior. When your big feathery friend comes lookin’ for you, my net gun’ll do the rest.” He patted a sinister-looking contraption mounted on the truck’s cab.

As McLeach turned to check his gear, Cody’s mind raced. He saw a sharp, rusty edge of the truck’s side panel near his bound hands. Carefully, slowly, he began to saw the rope against it.

High above, unseen by McLeach, a single golden feather drifted on the thermals. Faloo, with Nelson the koala clinging to his back, had followed at a safe distance. They watched from a high rock ledge.

“We gotta do something, Faloo!” Nelson whispered, his nose twitching in anxiety.

“We will, mate,” Faloo said, his usual humor gone. “But we need the others. We need a proper mob.”

Back in the truck, Cody worked furiously. The fibers of the rope began to fray. He glanced at McLeach, who was now scanning the skies with binoculars. Just a little more.

A powerful, familiar cry echoed through the canyon. *Kreee-ee-ee!*

Marahute was circling high above, her golden wings catching the last of the sun.

McLeach spun around, a vicious smile spreading across his face. “Right on time.” He grabbed the net gun.

The rope snapped. Cody’s hands were free. He fumbled with the knots at his ankles.

Marahute saw her little friend. With a scream of fury, she folded her wings and dove.

“That’s it, come to mama…” McLeach whispered, taking aim.

Just as Marahute entered range, a chaotic noise erupted. From every crack in the rocks, from behind every scrubby bush, the animal mob charged. Wombats rolled like furry boulders, lizards darted underfoot, birds dove at McLeach’s head in a shrieking cloud.

“What the—! Get off!” McLeach yelled, swatting blindly, his aim ruined.

In the confusion, Cody freed his ankles. He scrambled to the edge of the truck bed as Marahute pulled out of her dive, her talons outstretched for him.

McLeach saw him. “Oh no you don’t!” He lunged, grabbing Cody’s shirt.

For a second, they struggled, Cody dangling over the side. Then, with a final, mighty pull, Cody tore free, his shirt ripping in McLeach’s hand.

He fell.

And was caught.

Marahute’s talons closed gently around him, lifting him skyward. Below, McLeach roared in frustration, lost in a swarm of angry wildlife.

Safe in the eagle’s grasp, Cody looked down at the shrinking scene, then up at Marahute’s fierce, protective eye. He let out a breath he didn’t know he was holding. They flew, not towards the nest, but towards the warm lights of his home, where supper—and a very worried mom—was waiting.

response:
**Actor Taraji P. Henson Alleges Son Was Racially Profiled by Police at USC**

Academy Award-nominated actor Taraji P. Henson has publicly alleged that her adult son, Marcel, was the victim of racial profiling by police officers during a recent incident at the University of Southern California (USC). The claim has ignited a conversation about policing and bias, prompting a direct response from the university’s head of public safety, who has shared his own experiences with racial profiling.

Henson, known for her powerful roles in films like *The Curious Case of Benjamin Button* and the television series *Empire*, revealed the allegation in a public statement. She described the stop of her son as an unsettling example of the racial profiling that disproportionately affects Black men and boys across the United States. While specific details of the encounter have not been fully disclosed, Henson’s decision to speak out highlights the personal fear and frustration felt by many families.

“No parent should have to worry that their child will be targeted simply because of the color of their skin,” Henson’s statement read in part. “This was a frightening and unnecessary ordeal for my son, and it’s a painful reminder of the work that still needs to be done.”

The incident reportedly occurred on or near the USC campus, where Marcel Henson is a student. The university, a private research institution in Los Angeles, has its own Department of Public Safety (DPS), which employs sworn police officers with full arrest powers. The involvement of this specific department places the allegation squarely within the university’s purview.

In a significant and immediate response, USC’s Chief of Public Safety, John Thomas, addressed the allegation. Chief Thomas, who leads the DPS, did not comment on the specifics of the ongoing review but made a powerful personal admission. He revealed that he himself has been a victim of racial profiling, providing a stark perspective from within law enforcement leadership.

“As a Black man in America, I have personally experienced the sting of racial profiling,” Thomas stated. “Those experiences inform my commitment to fair and equitable policing. We take these allegations with the utmost seriousness.”

Following Henson’s public allegation, Chief Thomas has vowed to conduct a thorough, independent investigation into the matter. He emphasized that the review would examine all aspects of the police stop, including the officers’ actions and the rationale behind the encounter. The pledge for a transparent investigation is seen as a critical step in addressing the community’s concerns.

“I have directed my staff to carry out a complete and impartial investigation into this incident,” Thomas said. “Our community deserves answers, and we are committed to ensuring that our policies and practices are applied justly and without bias.”

The allegation from a high-profile figure like Taraji P. Henson brings renewed national attention to the ongoing issue of racial profiling in policing. It also places USC, a major university in a diverse city, under a microscope regarding its own public safety protocols and culture. Students and activists have long called for greater accountability and reformed policing practices on college campuses and in communities nationwide.

Community leaders and civil rights advocates have echoed Henson’s concerns, noting that such incidents, whether involving a celebrity’s child or not, are emblematic of a systemic problem. They argue that proactive measures, continuous training on implicit bias, and independent oversight are essential to rebuilding trust between law enforcement and the communities they serve.

As the investigation by USC’s Department of Public Safety gets underway, the campus community and the public await its findings. The outcome will likely influence discussions on police reform at the university level and beyond. For Taraji P. Henson and her family, the hope is that speaking out will lead to concrete change, ensuring the safety and dignity of all students.

The university has stated it will share relevant findings from the investigation upon its conclusion, in line with legal and privacy constraints. For now, the case stands as a poignant reminder of the personal impact of racial bias and the ongoing struggle for equality within the justice system.

He emphasized that the review would examine all aspects of the police stop, including the officers’ actions and the rationale behind the encounter. The pledge for a transparent investigation is seen as a critical step in addressing the community’s concerns.

“I have directed my staff to carry out a complete and impartial investigation into this incident,” Thomas said. “Our community deserves answers, and we are committed to ensuring that our policies and practices are applied justly and without bias.”

The allegation from a high-profile figure like Taraji P. Henson brings renewed national attention to the ongoing issue of racial profiling in policing. It also places USC, a major university in a diverse city, under a microscope regarding its own public safety protocols and culture. Students and activists have long called for greater accountability and reformed policing practices on college campuses and in communities nationwide.

Community leaders and civil rights advocates have echoed Henson’s concerns, noting that such incidents, whether involving a celebrity’s child or not, are emblematic of a systemic problem. They argue that proactive measures, continuous training on implicit bias, and independent oversight are essential to rebuilding trust between law enforcement and the communities they serve.

As the investigation by USC’s Department of Public Safety gets underway, the campus community and the public await its findings. The outcome will likely influence discussions on police reform at the university level and beyond. For Taraji P. Henson and her family, the hope is that speaking out will lead to concrete change, ensuring the safety and dignity of all students.

The university has stated it will share relevant findings from the investigation upon its conclusion, in line with legal and privacy constraints. For now, the case stands as a poignant reminder of the personal impact of racial bias and the ongoing struggle for equality within the justice system.
